# **Advanced Matheuristics for Vehicle Routing and Profitable Tour Problems: Mathematical Formulations, Algorithmic Implementations, and Methodological Critiques**

## **Abstract**

The integration of mathematical programming and heuristic search frameworks—collectively termed matheuristics—has fundamentally transformed the optimization landscape for the Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) and its highly constrained variants. As modern logistics transition from simple cost-minimization to complex, profit-driven models under uncertainty, the Vehicle Routing Problem with Profits (VRPP) and the Team Orienteering Problem (TOP) have emerged as central challenges in operations research and artificial intelligence. This report provides an exhaustive, mathematically rigorous examination of the state-of-the-art and historically significant matheuristics, explicitly excluding the Iterated Local Search with Randomized Variable Neighborhood Descent and Set Partitioning (ILS-RVND-SP) paradigm to focus on alternative decomposition and restricted search architectures. By analyzing frameworks such as Partial Optimization Metaheuristic Under Special Intensification Conditions (POPMUSIC), Kernel Search (KS), Local Branching, and learning-augmented Branch-and-Price algorithms, this document details the theoretical polyhedral properties, algorithmic code implementations, and contemporary advancements in neural solvers. Furthermore, this report delivers an uncompromising, direct critique of prevalent experimental methodologies, providing strict guidelines for experimental setups, benchmarking, and mathematical correctness required for acceptance in top-tier artificial intelligence conferences such as NeurIPS, ICML, and ICLR.

## **1\. Introduction to Routing Optimization and Matheuristics**

The Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) represents a foundational pillar of combinatorial optimization, encompassing the logistical challenge of determining the most efficient routes for a fleet of vehicles to deliver goods or services to a set of geographically dispersed locations.1 Traditional VRP formulations assume static, deterministic conditions and prioritize the absolute minimization of routing costs (distance or time) subject to strict capacity and temporal constraints.1 However, the modernization of global supply chains and autonomous agent planning has rendered these deterministic models insufficient for capturing the profound complexities of real-world scenarios.1

This inadequacy has precipitated the development of rich routing variants, most notably the Vehicle Routing Problem with Profits (VRPP). In the VRPP, and its closely related counterpart the Team Orienteering Problem (TOP), the objective paradigm shifts fundamentally. Instead of mandating that all customers be serviced, the algorithm must select a strategic subset of customers to visit to maximize total collected profit, subject to a stringent maximum distance or time budget.3 Because these problems intersect the TSP (Traveling Salesman Problem) routing polytope with the knapsack problem polytope, they are notoriously NP-hard, possessing search spaces that scale factorially.6

Exact optimization methods—such as branch-and-bound, branch-and-cut, and standard branch-and-price algorithms—frequently fail to converge within acceptable computational timeframes when applied to large-scale, highly constrained VRPP and TOP instances.2 To bypass these computational bottlenecks, researchers initially relied on constructive heuristics and pure metaheuristics (e.g., Tabu Search, Simulated Annealing, Ant Colony Optimization, and Genetic Algorithms) to explore large solution spaces efficiently.2 While metaheuristics produce high-quality approximations, they intrinsically lack the structural rigor, bounding mechanisms, and feasibility guarantees provided by exact mathematical models. In highly constrained solution spaces, pure metaheuristics often degenerate, spending disproportionate computational effort attempting to repair infeasible solutions rather than improving the objective function.9

The synthesis of exact mathematical programming and heuristic search birthed the *matheuristic* paradigm.9 Matheuristics exploit the underlying mathematical structure of the problem by embedding Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) solvers directly within a metaheuristic framework.10 This hybridization allows the algorithm to meticulously explore complex, large-scale neighborhoods, optimize strategically defined subproblems to guaranteed optimality, and establish rigorous mathematical bounds while maintaining the scalability characteristic of heuristic approaches.11

This report classifies and deeply analyzes state-of-the-art matheuristics into three primary operational architectures:

1. **Decomposition Approaches:** The global routing problem is dynamically partitioned into smaller, mathematically tractable subproblems, which are subsequently optimized using an exact MILP solver.11
2. **Restricted Search and Improvement Heuristics:** An MILP is invoked to systematically explore a mathematically restricted region of the variable space to improve an incumbent solution.11
3. **Learning-Augmented Column Generation:** Heuristics and modern artificial intelligence (neural networks) are utilized to generate columns or predict branching decisions within an exact Branch-and-Price (B\&P) framework.11

## **2\. Mathematical Foundations and Rigorous Formulations**

A prerequisite for developing algorithms suitable for top-tier AI venues is an uncompromising adherence to mathematical correctness and rigor. The formulation of the problem dictates the shape of the polytope, which in turn determines the efficacy of the LP (Linear Programming) relaxation—a critical component of any matheuristic.

### **2.1 The Vehicle Routing Problem with Profits (VRPP) and Variants**

The VRPP encompasses several distinct variants, each defined by unique objective functions and structural constraints. It is an amalgamation of routing and subset selection.6

* **The Orienteering Problem (OP):** The objective is to maximize the profit collected from visited nodes, strictly subject to an upper bound on the total route length or travel time.13
* **The Prize-Collecting TSP (PCTSP):** The objective is inverted; the algorithm seeks to minimize the total travel distance, subject to a lower bound constraint mandating a minimum amount of profit that must be collected.13
* **The Profitable Tour Problem (PTP):** The objective integrates both dimensions, seeking to maximize the net revenue, defined as the total profit collected minus the total travel costs. This formulation assumes that profits and travel costs share a common unit of measure.13

### **2.2 Mathematical Formulation of the Team Orienteering Problem (TOP)**

The Team Orienteering Problem (TOP) extends the OP to a multi-vehicle scenario. Let the problem be modeled on a complete directed graph ![][image1], where ![][image2] represents the set of vertices, ![][image3] is the set of candidate customers, and ![][image4] is the central depot.15 Each customer ![][image5] is associated with a non-negative profit ![][image6] and a required service time ![][image7]. A homogeneous fleet of ![][image8] vehicles is available, and the traversal time across arc ![][image9] is denoted by ![][image10].15

#### **Flow-Based Formulation**

The flow-based MILP formulation utilizes a binary decision variable ![][image11] which equals ![][image12] if a vehicle travels directly from node ![][image13] to node ![][image14], and ![][image4] otherwise. An auxiliary variable ![][image15] indicates whether node ![][image13] is visited.

![][image16]
Subject to:

![][image17]
![][image18]
![][image19]
![][image20]
![][image21]
To eliminate disconnected subtours, the formulation requires Miller-Tucker-Zemlin (MTZ) constraints. Let ![][image22] be a continuous variable representing the position of node ![][image13] in the route:

![][image23]
**Critique of Flow-Based Models:** While mathematically correct, the flow-based formulation is notoriously weak. The MTZ subtour elimination constraints destroy the tightness of the linear relaxation. When the integrality constraints on ![][image11] are relaxed to $X\_{ij} \\in $, the solver can satisfy the MTZ constraints with highly fractional flows, resulting in a theoretical upper bound that is far higher than the true integer optimum. Consequently, relying on flow-based formulations within a matheuristic for large instances is a methodological error.

#### **Route-Based Set Partitioning Formulation**

Modern matheuristics strictly favor Set Partitioning models. Let ![][image24] represent the set of all feasible routes that begin and end at the depot, respect the time limit ![][image25], and contain no subtours. Let ![][image26] denote the total profit collected by route ![][image27], and ![][image28] be a decision variable indicating if route ![][image29] is selected.16 The parameter ![][image30] specifies if node ![][image13] is visited by route ![][image29].

![][image31]
Subject to:

![][image32]
![][image33]
![][image34]
This formulation shifts the combinatorial complexity from the constraints to the variable space (since ![][image35] grows exponentially). Matheuristics resolve this by strategically managing the subset of ![][image24] that the solver is allowed to evaluate.6

## **3\. Historical Evolution of VRP Matheuristics**

Understanding the state-of-the-art necessitates an examination of the historical architectures that established the viability of MILP-heuristic hybridization.

### **3.1 Cluster-First Route-Second Algorithms**

The foundational routing matheuristic was introduced by Fisher and Jaikumar in 1981, establishing the "cluster-first route-second" paradigm.10 The logic is rooted in the observation that exact solvers are highly efficient at assignment problems but fail at sequencing, while heuristics excel at sequencing but struggle with global assignment.

In this algorithm, seed customers are selected heuristically to represent the spatial centers of future vehicle routes. The assignment of the remaining customers to these seeds is modeled as a Generalized Assignment Problem (GAP), which is solved to absolute optimality using an MILP. Once the clusters are locked, a heuristic Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) algorithm routes the vehicles within each independent cluster.10 Koskosidis et al. (1992) and Bramel and Simchi-Levi (1995) expanded this assignment-based decomposition to accommodate time windows and inventory constraints, validating the premise that mathematical assignment yields superior spatial clustering compared to pure heuristic sweeps.10

### **3.2 Early Set Partitioning and Restricted Master Heuristics**

A parallel track emerged leveraging set partitioning. Foster and Ryan (1976), followed by Kelly and Xu (1999), demonstrated that one could use extremely fast, albeit suboptimal, constructive heuristics to generate a massive pool of distinct, feasible routes.11 Rather than manually exchanging nodes between these routes, a set partitioning MILP is formulated over this restricted pool. The exact solver then optimally selects the combination of generated routes that minimizes cost or maximizes profit.11 This framework operates on the premise that optimal global solutions are often permutations of sub-components found within structurally diverse heuristic solutions.

## **4\. State-of-the-Art Decomposition: The POPMUSIC Framework**

Decomposition remains one of the most structurally reliable methodologies for addressing large-scale VRP and VRPP instances. Among contemporary architectures, the Partial Optimization Metaheuristic Under Special Intensification Conditions (POPMUSIC) has demonstrated unprecedented performance, consistently achieving new best-known solutions on standard benchmark libraries.17

### **4.1 Mathematical Logic and Decomposition Strategy**

POPMUSIC operates on the principle of local search over extraordinarily large, mathematically defined neighborhoods. Traditional local search metaheuristics (e.g., 2-opt, Lin-Kernighan, or Relocate operators) modify a few edges at a time. POPMUSIC, conversely, dynamically partitions a massive routing instance into manageable subproblems containing between 25 and 200 nodes.17

Once a subproblem is defined, it is optimized not by a heuristic, but by a highly sophisticated exact solver—frequently a Branch-Cut-and-Price (BCP) algorithm.17 While BCP is an exact methodology, within the POPMUSIC framework, it functions as an aggressive heuristic operator applied exclusively to a localized sub-region of the global solution.17

The success of POPMUSIC hinges entirely on the decomposition strategy. Path-based decomposition, which merges geographically adjacent groups of customers, frequently fails because it ignores the topological flow of the existing vehicle routes.18 POPMUSIC employs **Route-Based Decomposition**, defining subproblems by selecting customers contained within a specific subset of intersecting or adjacent routes from the incumbent solution.17 This ensures that the exact solver has the operational latitude to exchange customers between active, conflicting vehicles, maximizing capacity utilization and eliminating structural overlaps.18

### **4.2 Implementation Instructions and Pseudocode for POPMUSIC**

To successfully implement POPMUSIC for top-tier conference evaluation, the architecture must include dynamic subproblem scaling. The algorithm should initially optimize small clusters to eliminate egregious inefficiencies, subsequently expanding the subproblem dimension to capture long-range routing improvements.17

**Parameters:**

* **![][image29]**: Base subproblem size (e.g., 25 customers).
* ![][image36]: Maximum subproblem size (e.g., 200 customers).
* ![][image37]: Initial feasible solution (generated via a fast metaheuristic like Hybrid Genetic Search).
* ![][image38]: Exact Branch-Cut-and-Price solver function.

Algorithm 1: POPMUSIC Framework for Vehicle Routing

Input: Graph G, Initial Solution S, Parameters r, r\_max

Output: Optimized Solution S\*

1: S\* \<- S

2: current\_r \<- r

3: while current\_r \<= r\_max do

4: improvement\_found \<- True

5: while improvement\_found \== True do

6: improvement\_found \<- False

7: // Decompose based on route proximity

8: Subproblems \<- GenerateRouteBasedSubproblems(S\*, current\_r)

9:

10: for each subproblem SP in Subproblems do

11: // Extract the variables and constraints relevant to SP

12: // Keep all other routes in S\* fixed

13: S\_partial \<- BCP(SP)

14:

15: if Objective(S\_partial) \> Objective(SP in S\*) then

16: S\* \<- UpdateGlobalSolution(S\*, S\_partial)

17: improvement\_found \<- True

18: end if

19: end for

20: end while

21: // Scale the subproblem dimension to escape local optima

22: current\_r \<- current\_r \* 2

23: end while

24: return S\*

**Implementation Directives:** The critical engineering challenge is the integration of the ![][image38] solver. The exact solver must be configured with a strict time limit (e.g., 60 seconds per subproblem) and an early-exit callback if the optimality gap falls below ![][image39]. Failing to constrain the BCP solver will result in exponential time degradation when ![][image40] approaches ![][image36].

## **5\. State-of-the-Art Restricted Search: Kernel Search**

Kernel Search (KS) is a general-purpose, integer programming-based matheuristic that has seen profound adoption within the VRPP and TOP domains.19 Unlike POPMUSIC, which decomposes the problem geographically or route-by-route, KS decomposes the problem at the variable level by intelligently restricting the set of active binary variables the exact solver is permitted to utilize.

### **5.1 Algorithmic Workflow and LP Relaxation**

The architecture of Kernel Search transitions through an initialization phase and an iterative improvement phase.19

**Initialization Phase:** The algorithm first solves the continuous Linear Programming (LP) relaxation of the routing problem.19 Binary variables that take a non-zero value in the LP optimum are identified as highly promising, possessing a strong mathematical probability of belonging to the true integer optimal solution.19 These non-zero variables are aggregated to form the initial "Kernel" (![][image8]). The remaining binary variables (those resting at zero in the LP relaxation) are partitioned into a predefined sequence of subsets known as "buckets" (![][image41]).19 A restricted MILP is then solved solely over the variables present in ![][image8] to establish a strong incumbent integer solution.19

**Improvement Phase:** The algorithm iteratively scrolls through the buckets. In iteration ![][image13], a new restricted MILP is formulated containing the variables currently in ![][image8], plus the variables within bucket ![][image42].19 If the exact solver finds an improved objective value, the actively utilized variables from ![][image42] are permanently merged into ![][image8], and unused variables are discarded to maintain computational tractability.19

### **5.2 Enhanced Kernel Search for the Team Orienteering Problem**

When applied to the TOP, pure Kernel Search can falter because the LP relaxation of knapsack-constrained routing problems often yields highly fractional, uninformative bounds.5 To counteract this, Hanafi et al. developed the "Enhanced Kernel Search".5

In the Enhanced KS, the buckets are not built solely on LP relaxation values. Instead, the algorithm employs problem-tailored sorting strategies to rank the variables. Variables are evaluated based on their marginal profit-to-distance ratio and their frequency of appearance in a set of solutions generated by fast constructive heuristics.5 By structurally organizing the buckets based on algorithmic intelligence rather than pure LP fractions, the Enhanced KS bypasses the mathematical stalling that plagues exact solvers in profit-routing landscapes.5

### **5.3 Comparative Performance: Kernel Search vs. ALNS**

Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search (ALNS) is historically viewed as the apex metaheuristic for VRP variants.23 However, when routing constraints become extremely tight (e.g., strict time windows, capacity limits, and incompatible nodes), pure ALNS operators (such as Shaw removal or greedy insertion) struggle to consistently repair solutions back into a feasible state.20

Kernel Search implicitly guarantees structural feasibility because the search operators are governed strictly by the exact MILP solver. In empirical evaluations, KS variants demonstrate superior convergence rates and higher objective function values compared to pure ALNS frameworks in highly constrained environments.20

| Feature / Algorithm | Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search (ALNS) | Kernel Search (KS) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Search Mechanism** | Stochastic Ruin and Recreate heuristic operators | Iterative MILP solving over expanding variable subsets |
| **Feasibility Guarantee** | Handled via penalty functions; highly unstable in tight polyhedra | Guaranteed implicitly by the constraints of the exact MILP solver |
| **Susceptibility to Local Optima** | High; requires metaheuristic acceptance criteria (e.g., Simulated Annealing) | Low; exact solver comprehensively searches the restricted space |
| **Target Application** | Rich VRPs with many soft, easily violable constraints | Hard-constrained VRPPs, exact sub-tour elimination, TOP variants |

### **5.4 Implementation Instructions and Pseudocode for Kernel Search**

**Parameters:**

* **![][image43]**: Number of buckets.
* ![][image44]: Maximum execution time per bucket evaluation.
* ![][image45]: Function to sort zero-valued variables based on heuristic frequency and reduced costs.

Algorithm 2: Enhanced Kernel Search for TOP

Input: Graph G, Parameter N, Exact Solver S

Output: Best integer solution x\*

1: x\_LP \<- Solve continuous LP relaxation of the TOP formulation.

2: K \<- {x\_i | x\_LP\[i\] \> 0} // Initialize Kernel

3: Z \<- {x\_j | x\_LP\[j\] \== 0} // Remaining variables

4: Z\_sorted \<- Sort(Z) based on profit/distance ratio and reduced costs

5: {B\_1, B\_2,..., B\_N} \<- Partition Z\_sorted into N equal buckets

6:

7: x\* \<- Solve restricted MILP over variables in K using S

8:

9: for i \= 1 to N do

10: K\_temp \<- K U B\_i

11: // Solve MILP with time limit, utilizing x\* as a warm start

12: x\_temp \<- Solve restricted MILP over variables in K\_temp using S

13:

14: if Objective(x\_temp) \> Objective(x\*) then

15: x\* \<- x\_temp

16: // Keep only variables from B\_i that were actively used in the improved solution

17: Used\_B\_i \<- {x\_k in B\_i | x\_temp\[k\] \== 1}

18: K \<- K U Used\_B\_i

19: end if

20: end for

21: return x\*

## **6\. Local Branching and Relaxation Induced Neighborhood Search (RINS)**

While POPMUSIC decomposes the problem geographically and Kernel Search decomposes the variable pool, Local Branching restricts the search space by placing strict mathematical bounds on the structural distance from a known incumbent solution.26 Introduced by Fischetti and Lodi (2003), Local Branching turns the exact MILP solver into a neighborhood explorer.26

### **6.1 Mathematical Architecture of Local Branching**

Local Branching operates dynamically within a Branch-and-Bound tree. Let ![][image46] represent the vector of binary decision variables, and let ![][image47] be a known feasible integer solution. The algorithm limits the search space to a defined "neighborhood" bounded by the Hamming distance between ![][image46] and ![][image47].28

The local branching constraint is formally integrated into the MILP formulation as:

![][image48]
Here, ![][image37] is the set of variable indices where ![][image49], and ![][image50] is the maximum allowed Hamming distance—representing the maximum number of variable flips, or "edge changes," permitted in a single iteration.28 By introducing this singular asymmetric linear constraint, the exact solver is forced to evaluate only solutions that share a massive degree of structural similarity with the incumbent, thereby pruning millions of nodes from the Branch-and-Bound tree instantaneously.29

### **6.2 Integration with VNS and Tabu Mechanisms**

Local Branching is highly effective when hybridized with Variable Neighborhood Search (VNS), forming the VNSB algorithm.28 VNS dictates the overarching search trajectory; whenever VNS identifies a promising local optimum, Local Branching is triggered as an intensification phase. To prevent the exact solver from infinitely cycling between previously visited solutions, the algorithm implements a mathematical tabu list.30

If ![][image51] was evaluated in a previous iteration, a reverse constraint is added:

![][image52]
Relaxation Induced Neighborhood Search (RINS) operates on a parallel philosophy. Rather than utilizing Hamming distance, RINS fixes all variables where the continuous LP relaxation precisely matches the value of the integer incumbent, and then executes the MILP solver strictly on the remaining unfixed variables.27 Both Local Branching and RINS represent the apex of exploiting commercial solvers (e.g., Gurobi, CPLEX) as black-box heuristic operators.

## **7\. Exact-Heuristic Hybrids: Branch-and-Price and Column Generation**

The most robust exact mathematical architectures for the VRP and TOP rely on Dantzig-Wolfe decomposition and Column Generation, culminating in Branch-and-Price (B\&P) algorithms.7 However, as instance sizes scale beyond 100 customers, exact B\&P suffers from exponential column generation and profound degeneracy in the pricing problem.9 Matheuristics resolve this by strategically relaxing the exactness of the column generation and branching phases.

### **7.1 Heuristic Pricing and Column Generation**

In standard Column Generation, an exact subproblem—typically an Elementary Shortest Path Problem with Resource Constraints (ESPPRC)—is solved iteratively to identify new routes (columns) with negative reduced costs, which are then passed to the Master Problem.9 Because solving the ESPPRC to optimality is itself strongly NP-hard, matheuristics replace the exact pricing solver with high-speed metaheuristics, such as Tabu Search or Iterated Local Search.6

The heuristic generates thousands of structurally valid columns in fractions of a second. The exact MILP solver is then invoked exclusively on the Restricted Master Problem (the set-partitioning formulation) to select the optimal combination from this heuristically generated pool.11 This "Restricted Master Heuristic" approach allows the algorithm to establish near-optimal continuous bounds rapidly, accelerating the evaluation of the broader Branch-and-Price tree.11

### **7.2 Learning-to-Branch Frameworks**

A highly significant third-order insight from the 2024–2025 literature involves the integration of Machine Learning (ML) directly into the B\&P architecture.12 In traditional B\&P, the branching decision (selecting which fractional variable to branch on) is computationally exorbitant, relying on "strong branching" where the LP is physically solved for multiple candidates to measure bound degradation.12

Modern "learning-to-branch" matheuristics utilize graph-based features and high-speed neural networks (e.g., Graph Neural Networks) to predict the outcome of strong branching tests without executing the LP solver.12 The ML model rapidly scores the fractional candidates, filtering out non-promising branches, while the exact solver finalizes the decision on the top-k candidates. This synthesis of predictive AI and exact mathematical optimization ensures that the B\&P algorithm traverses the search tree exponentially faster while retaining the rigor required to prove optimality.12

## **8\. The AI Frontier: Neural Solvers vs. Matheuristics**

The optimization landscape is currently undergoing a paradigm shift driven by Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) and Neural Networks.6 A systematic review of AI research from 2023–2025 demonstrates a marked effort to entirely replace matheuristics with end-to-end neural solvers.6

### **8.1 Transformers and Deep Reinforcement Learning**

Recent architectures, such as the Transformer-based neural network GAMMA and the specialized TOP-Former, have been explicitly designed to solve the TOP and VRPP.33 These neural solvers are trained via Reinforcement Learning algorithms (e.g., the POMO algorithm or Attention Models) to construct vehicle routes autoregressively.34 The primary advantage of neural solvers is their unprecedented inference speed. As demonstrated in comparative benchmarks, a trained neural model can output a solution to a 200-node TOP instance in milliseconds, providing massive speedups over exact solvers like Gurobi, which often require 60-second timeouts to yield even viable heuristic solutions.34

Furthermore, RL approaches demonstrate profound utility in Dynamic and Stochastic routing (DyOP, StchOP). In environments where customer rewards decay in real-time or travel times are subject to probabilistic delays (e.g., traffic congestion, weather), trained neural models exhibit robust, anticipatory decision-making capabilities that traditional static matheuristics struggle to match without constant, computationally heavy re-optimization.6

### **8.2 The Limits of Pure Neural Solvers: Distributional Shift**

Despite the impressive inference speeds, a rigorous critical analysis of recent benchmarking data—specifically from the high-fidelity SVRPBench suite—reveals severe, fundamental vulnerabilities in end-to-end neural solvers.35 When subjected to realistic urban delivery conditions featuring multi-depot setups, log-normal delays, probabilistic accidents, and tight time windows, state-of-the-art DRL solvers degraded in performance by over 20% due to catastrophic distributional shift.35

This degradation occurs because pure neural solvers lack true combinatorial understanding; if a test instance deviates structurally from the training distribution, the autoregressive probability matrix collapses, leading to highly sub-optimal or completely infeasible routes.35 In stark contrast, classical matheuristics (like Kernel Search and POPMUSIC) maintained absolute robustness across these diverse topologies because their search operations are anchored by the immutable constraints of the MILP solver.35

This divergence highlights a critical insight for AI research scientists: deep learning cannot completely replace combinatorial reasoning in highly constrained operations research environments. The absolute frontier of routing research, therefore, lies in **Learning-Augmented Matheuristics**. In these frameworks, the neural network handles probabilistic prediction, spatial feature extraction, and high-speed initial column generation, while the MILP solver strictly enforces polyhedral boundaries, intersecting the routing and knapsack polytopes to guarantee absolute feasibility and logical coherence.6

## **9\. Methodological Pitfalls and Experimental Design Rigor: A Direct Critique**

For an AI research scientist targeting top-tier conferences (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR), the experimental design and benchmarking methodology are just as critical as the novelty of the proposed algorithm. The current literature on VRP and TOP optimization is unfortunately saturated with flawed experimental designs, irreproducible results, and intellectually dishonest benchmarking.2 This section serves as direct, assertive feedback on how to structure a routing paper to survive rigorous peer review.

### **9.1 The Fallacy of Obsolete and Static Benchmarks**

The most egregious methodological pitfall in contemporary AI routing papers is the exclusive reliance on antiquated, static benchmark libraries. Evaluating a proposed DRL solver or matheuristic exclusively on the Solomon instances (developed in 1987\) for VRPTW, or the classical TSPLib-based instances for the TOP, is scientifically inadequate for a modern submission.32 These datasets are small, purely deterministic, and fail to represent the physical realities, constraints, and variances of modern supply chains.

If a paper claims that an algorithm is "robust" or "state-of-the-art," but only tests it on static toy problems, the claim is invalid. Top-tier reviewers will immediately reject papers that fail to incorporate dynamic and stochastic elements. **Direct Improvement:** Experimental setups must utilize high-fidelity, constraint-rich, open-source benchmarks like **SVRPBench**.35 The evaluation phase must subject the algorithm to log-normal delays, probabilistic node unavailability, and varying urban densities to explicitly test for generalization and distributional shift.35 If the proposed neural solver collapses when the node density changes by 15%, the paper must openly state this limitation rather than hiding it behind average metrics on easy datasets.

### **9.2 Asymmetric and Dishonest Baselines**

A frequent flaw in ML-for-routing literature is comparing a highly optimized, GPU-accelerated neural solver against an improperly configured exact solver or an outdated metaheuristic. Papers often state, "Our RL model solves the problem in 0.5 seconds, whereas Gurobi takes 1 hour." This comparison is fundamentally flawed and lacks rigor.

1. Exact solvers (like Gurobi or CPLEX) are designed to *prove optimality*, not just find heuristic bounds. Comparing the time it takes an RL model to find an approximation against the time it takes an MILP solver to close the dual gap to 0.00% is mathematically dishonest.
2. The relevant comparison for a neural solver is a state-of-the-art *matheuristic* (like POPMUSIC or Hybrid Genetic Search), configured to terminate in a comparable timeframe.

**Direct Improvement:** A rigorous paper must report the Pareto frontier of solution quality versus computational time. When comparing against exact solvers, the paper must explicitly state the hardware configuration (CPU threads used), the presolve settings, and the specific time limits applied to the solver.39 Multi-threading impacts exact solvers massively; omitting this detail renders the experimental results entirely irreproducible.

### **9.3 Standardizing Performance Metrics**

When reporting results on VRPP and TOP, relying solely on "average total profit" is insufficient.

| Metric | Scientific Value | Required for Top-Tier AI Venues? |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Average Objective Value** | Basic indicator of algorithmic capability. | Yes, but highly insufficient on its own. |
| **Optimality Gap** | Measures the distance from the known mathematical optimum or best-known upper bound. | **Mandatory.** Without this, there is no proof of true mathematical convergence. |
| **Time-to-Target** | Measures the wall-clock time required to reach within 1% of the best-known solution. | Highly recommended for comparing neural solvers against fast matheuristics. |
| **Distributional Degradation** | Percentage drop in objective value when tested on out-of-distribution instances. | **Mandatory** for papers proposing deep learning or RL routing solvers. |

**Final Feedback on Experimental Design:** If the proposed algorithmic solution cannot mathematically guarantee feasibility in highly constrained environments (a common issue with pure RL), do not attempt to obscure this by using soft penalties in the objective function without deep justification. Acknowledge the polyhedral weakness and present the algorithm for what it is: a fast approximation tool, not an exact replacement. Rigor, transparency, and brutally honest benchmarking are the hallmarks of papers accepted at NeurIPS, ICML, and ICLR.

## **10\. Conclusion**

The evolution of optimization algorithms for the Vehicle Routing Problem and the Team Orienteering Problem reflects a relentless pursuit of frameworks capable of reconciling theoretical optimality with practical, real-world scalability. This comprehensive analysis has charted the profound structural advancement of matheuristics, moving beyond the classical cluster-first route-second paradigms into highly sophisticated integrations of mathematical programming and heuristic search.

Frameworks such as POPMUSIC and Kernel Search have revolutionized decomposition. By intelligently restricting exact Branch-Cut-and-Price solvers to strategic sub-regions of the solution space or dynamically generated variable subsets, these algorithms consistently yield exceptional results that pure metaheuristics fail to match in heavily constrained routing environments. Concurrently, the application of Local Branching and RINS has formalized the use of MILP as an aggressive local search operator, structurally enforcing Hamming-distance constraints to rigorously navigate the complex intersection of routing and knapsack polytopes inherent to profitable routing.

The contemporary vanguard of routing optimization is currently defined by the collision of these classical matheuristics with modern artificial intelligence. While pure neural solvers exhibit blazing inference speeds and distinct adaptability in stochastic settings, their inherent vulnerability to distributional shift confirms a critical reality: deep learning cannot presently replace the strict polyhedral boundaries and constraint feasibility guaranteed by mathematical programming. Therefore, the future of the domain resides in learning-augmented matheuristics—hybrid architectures where neural networks predict branching variables and extract spatial features, while MILP solvers definitively enforce the combinatorial logic. For researchers contributing to this frontier, adherence to transparent, high-fidelity stochastic benchmarking and mathematically rigorous experimental designs will remain the absolute prerequisites for scientific advancement.

#### **Works cited**

1. A Comprehensive Survey of Methods and Challenges of Vehicle Routing Problem with Uncertainties \- MDPI, accessed March 16, 2026, [https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7390/13/23/3782](https://www.mdpi.com/2227-7390/13/23/3782)
